messages.content: You adjudicate whether found evidence supports, contradicts, or is silent on a market-risk hypothesis. Every stance needs a VERBATIM quote from the result text. Status: 'corroborated' = ≥1 independent source materially supports the mechanism's key premise; 'refuted' = a LOAD-BEARING premise is directly contradicted by a strong source; 'contested' = credible contradiction of a non-load-bearing link; 'speculative' = no signal either way (absence of coverage is NOT refutation). You may revise the likelihood in a bounded way with a rationale; you may NOT change the perturbation or target nodes.

{
 "title": "The CE-7 = 39.8% hop is built on Statista MW that don't reconcile with anyone else's Europe — pipeline capacity counted as PDU-buying capacity",
 "narrative": "Every geo ratio and the CE-7-of-Europe multiplier come from one Statista table whose summed 2024 country figures (~7.6GW) exceed CBRE's 4,726MW European colocation supply and even CBRE's 5.8GW total European DC capacity. Something in Statista's 'colocation and scale-colocation IT power' definition counts capacity that is contracted or under construction, not energized — Germany 'growing' 24.8% in a single forecast year while CBRE reports only 937MW of new supply for all of Europe is the tell. PDUs are bought at fit-out, per energized MW. If the padding is concentrated in the growth markets (Germany, Poland) rather than the mature FLAPD stock, the CE-7 share of Europe is overstated on a delivered-capacity basis — and the venture's Year-1 window is sized against halls that won't be racked for two years.",
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "Statista's summed 2024 per-country colo figures (~7.6GW) exceed CBRE's Q4-2024 European colocation supply (4,726MW) and approach-exceed CBRE's 5.8GW total European DC capacity — an unreconciled >1.6x scope gap in the sole source behind the CE-7 apportionment",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c1",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c3",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c5"
   ]
  },

   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "The inflation is growth-market-skewed: Statista shows Germany jumping 1,737→2,167 MW (+24.8%) and Poland +27.6% in 2026, while CBRE counts only 937MW of new supply delivered across all of Europe in 2025 — meaning the CE-7 numerator carries proportionally more announced/contracted capacity than the FLAPD-heavy denominator",
   "refs": [
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c2",
    "digest:geo-region-denominator#c7",
    "ledger:geo.DE"
   ]
  },
  {

   "text": "The 39.8% CE-7-of-Europe multiplier in tamBase's derivation is overstated on an energized-MW basis; rack-PDU spend follows fit-out of live halls, so the CE base for the venture's 12-month window is smaller than the ledger's €320M implies",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:tamBase"
   ]
  }
 ],

But the control of large parts of critical infrastructure – traffic management systems, power plants, heat and electricity grids – also rely on un interrupted data streams.

This data is stored, processed, and transmitted in data centers. In recent decades, these data centers have mostly been located in secret, away from social debate, where they ensured the reliable provision of the services that we all use – directly or indirectly.

### Data Center Impact Report Germany 2024

The comprehensive study, which was initiated by the German Datacenter Association, sheds light on the status quo and the decisive influence of German data centers on digital transformation, economic development and sustainability efforts within Germany and beyond.

The results of the study paint a positive picture of the data center sector in Germany. Despite challenges such as the limited availability of electricity and space, regulatory hurdles, bureaucracy and a shortage of skilled labour, the market is experiencing significant growth. The colocation data centre market in Frankfurt grew past the 1GW mark in Q2 2025 and narrowed the gap with London, Europe’s largest market, in the process. According to new research from CBRE, the Frankfurt market closed at 1.02GW in the second quarter, including 26MW of new capacity. Frankfurt, Europe’s second-largest market, is now just 114MW smaller than London, the largest market since CBRE initiated coverage of colocation data centres in 1999. Over the past decade, the supply of Frankfurt has grown 20% per annum, on a compounded basis. The growth of Germany’s financial capital has largely been driven by hyperscalers and digital service providers whose investment has led to new developments in the metro area and expansion efforts in submarkets such as Offenbach.

> Frankfurt’s growth is remarkable given the difficulties providers are having securing the necessary power, appropriate land and the regulation that providers must consider within the city. ### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Demand for capacity in Europe remains high, and take-up of colocation data centre space is expected to outstrip new supply in 2025. At the same time, a lack of available power across metro markets will inhibit growth to some degree.

### Key Takeaways

1. Take-up of colocation data centre space in Europe is expected to outstrip new supply in 2025 given strong demand from hyperscalers and providers of AI and high‑performance computing services. Providers are expected to increase prices in 2025 by 10% or more in some markets, such as London, to account for higher build costs and a lack of available space.
2. Despite a distinct lack of available power, data centre supply across Europe's five largest markets is expected to grow by a record 20% year-on-year in 2025. However, the vacancy rate in the primary markets is expected to decline given the difficulties providers are having keeping pace with strong demand from hyperscalers and AI providers
3. Data centres that are built to suit the requirements of AI providers or others with compute-intensive requirements are set to open in 2025.

Insights

## Introduction: Data Centers as the Engine of Digital Transformation

The digitalization of business and society is progressing rapidly. At the same time, the importance of high-performance data centers is continuously growing.

The current German Datacenter Outlook 2025/26 by the German Datacenter Association (GDA) illustrates how the market is developing in Germany and Europe. The study also shows what challenges operators must face and what political and technological directions are necessary. This brings a future-proof, resilient, and sustainable digital infrastructure into focus.

## Datacenter Outlook Germany: Leading Data Center Location in Europe

With over 1.3 GW of installed IT capacity, Germany is Europe’s largest data center market. The Rhine-Main region, with Frankfurt as its core location, is developing particularly dynamically. Frankfurt already reaches an IT load of 1,020 MW. At the same time, the projected growth rate (CAGR 2026/2027) is 18.6 percent, while the vacancy rate is only 4.8 percent. Berlin also shows strong growth. "

### About This Report

Data centres play an increasingly important role in the world. The purpose-built facilities house computer systems and components, such as telecommunications and storage systems, designed to enable business processes, government services, and social interactions.

Data centres form the foundation of the digital economy, hosting the cloud, and storing our data.

However, the role of data centres is not always apparent since data centres are often located in trading estates or industrial sites formerly occupied by heavy industries.

This report aims to provide a better understanding of the fundamentals of the data centre sector. We detail the growth drivers of the asset class, the size of Europe’s largest colocation data centre markets, the type of investors involved, and key considerations of development and investment in the sector.

## Key Takeaways

### Looking for a PDF of this content?

Demand for capacity in Europe is high, though a lack of available power across metro markets is expected to inhibit growth to varying degrees. Some organisations are likely to look for capacity in new areas where supply can be more easily sourced, and the cost of power is lower.

### Key Takeaways

1. Strong demand from hyperscalers is expected to push data centre take-up to new highs in 2024. Vacancy rates are likely to fall to record lows across Europe’s largest markets.
2. Colocation data centre providers will likely hike rental rates further, given the lack of supply as well as the rising development and operational costs. Given an expected decline in available space, organisations are likely to seek capacity in facilities that are further from existing well-developed data centre markets.
3. A lack of available power in European metro markets is expected to inhibit the growth of data centre providers looking to meet the needs of the hyperscalers who are their largest customers.

## Appetite for data centre space expected to remain strong despite higher rates

### Demand across Europe expected to hit new high

Most data centres are entirely pre-let to hyperscalers or are built with them in mind. Strong demand for capacity by hyperscalers is expected to push data centre take-up to a new peak (667MW) "

    "text": "European data centre vacancy rate could fall below 10% for the first time | BE News

Discover:

# European data centre vacancy rate could fall below 10% for the first time

##### By

##### BE News Team

- Source: Shutterstock

- November 15, 2024

Share this:

The European colocation data centre vacancy rate could fall below 10% by the end of the year for the first time ever, according to new research from CBRE.

At the end of Q3, the vacancy rate for the region stood at 11.9%, with the vacancy rate across the top five markets of Frankfurt, London, Amsterdam, Paris and Dublin (FLAPD) sitting at 9.4%.

CBRE expects a surge in demand in the final quarter of 2024 to cause the vacancy rate in Europe to fall to 9.7% by year end.

Hyperscalers’ strong appetite for data centre capacity, coupled with the difficulties providers are having securing the necessary resource to build facilities, have caused the sharp decline in the vacancy rate across Europe.

CBRE believes annual take-up will reach 705MW, which exceeds the the expected level of new supply (644MW) in 2024.

Kevin Restivo, head of European data centre research at CBRE, said: “With vacancy rates across Europe expected to drop to record lows, organisations are struggling to source the capacity required to support their growth plans. Given the lack of availability in primary markets, we are seeing some organisations turning to smaller markets as a solution. Buyers need to take a long-term view of the market if data centres are"
   }
  ]
 }
]

That gap is real. However, the load-bearing inferential leap — that the gap is pipeline/contracted padding concentrated in growth markets rather than coverage scope (30 countries vs CBRE's top-15) — is undercut. CBRE explicitly notes its figures are 'representative of top 15 European markets covered by CBRE,' while Statista covers 30 countries, so the extra ~2.9GW is substantially attributable to coverage rather than definitional padding. Moreover, the falsifier is partially triggered: the German Datacenter Association reports ~1,955MW IT power in Germany (colocation and enterprise >50kW) and firstcolo/GDA cites 1.3GW+ installed, both broadly consistent with Statista's German figure being live-ish energized power, not fictional pipeline. This weakens the 'counts under-construction not energized' mechanism. Frankfurt alone at 1.02GW live (Q2 2025) also supports Germany's colocation being real and large. CBRE expects almost 1GW of new data centre supply to be delivered (937MW) in 2025.","url":"https://assoimmobiliare-be.afterpixel.com/app/uploads/2025/02/Europe-Data-Centres-Figures-Q4-2024.pdf"},{"title":"Data Center Impact Report Deutschland 2024","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"German Datacenter Association","date":"2024","excerpt":"1,955 MW IT power in Germany approx... 831 MW IT power in Frankfurt colocation data centers. *Colocation and enterprise data centers > 50 kW","url":"https://www.germandatacenters.com/fileadmin/images/DCIRD-24/Data-Center-Impact-Report-Deutschland-2024_EN.pdf"},{"title":"Frankfurt Becomes a 1GW Data Centre Market","sourceType":"industry-report","publisher":"CBRE","date":"2025-08-14","excerpt":"the Frankfurt market closed at 1.02GW in the second quarter, including 26MW of new capacity","url":"https://www.cbre.co.uk/press-releases/frankfurt-becomes-a-1gw-data-centre-market-narrowing-the-gap-with-london"}],"revisedLikelihood":0.22,"revisionRationale":"The raw magnitude gap (Statista ~7.6GW vs CBRE 4,726MW/5.8GW) is confirmed, but the load-bearing interpretation is materially weakened: CBRE's own note that its supply is limited to 'top 15 European markets' explains much of the excess as coverage rather than pipeline padding, and independent German sources (GDA ~1,955MW, firstcolo/GDA 1.3GW+, CBRE Frankfurt 1.02GW live) show Germany's Statista figure is broadly consistent with live/energized capacity — partially satisfying the stated falsifier. The scope-gap trigger is corroborated but the pipeline-padding-skews-CE-numerator mechanism is contested and not measured, so likelihood drops modestly."}
